answer:
Drawing upon the profound guidance within the Final Revelation and the authentic traditions of the Messenger, I present the sacred scriptural foundations of the *Shahada*, articulated with the clarity and grace required to guide every soul toward the truth of Divine Oneness and the finality of Prophethood. These *ayat* stand as the steadfast pillars of our faith, providing the verbatim declarations that constitute the testimony of every believer [1, 2].

### **The First Component: *Lā ilāha illā-Allāh***

**1. Sūrah Muhammad (47:19)**
*   **Arabic Text:** فَاعْلَمْ أَنَّهُ لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا اللَّهُ
*   **English Translation:** "So know, [O Muhammad], that there is no deity except Allah..." [3].
*   **Note:** This *ayah* commands the pursuit of certain knowledge regarding God’s absolute uniqueness, establishing the intellectual and spiritual prerequisite for the first half of the *Shahada* [3].

**2. Sūrah as-Sāffāt (37:35)**
*   **Arabic Text:** لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا اللَّهُ
*   **English Translation:** "...'There is no deity but Allah'..." [4].
*   **Note:** This verse records the verbatim declaration of *tawhid* that defined the message of the prophets and served as the point of distinction for those who submitted to the truth [2, 4].

---

### **The Second Component: *Muhammadun Rasūlullāh***

**3. Sūrah al-Fath (48:29)**
*   **Arabic Text:** مُّحَمَّدٌ رَّسُولُ اللَّهِ
*   **English Translation:** "Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah..." [5].
*   **Note:** This *ayah* provides the direct scriptural affirmation of the Prophetic office, completing the testimony by linking the recognition of the Creator to the acceptance of His final representative [5, 6].

---

**Scholarly Insight on the Scriptural Unity of the Testimony**
The *Shahada* represents the "word of righteousness" imposed upon the believers, acting as a "trustworthy handhold with no break in it" [7, 8]. While the two components appear in these distinct *ayat*, the Quran often presents them thematically as a single, inseparable truth: that the worship of God must be conducted according to the guidance revealed through His Messenger [6, 9]. As the mother of the believers, Aisha (may Allah be pleased with her), noted, the Prophet’s character was a living embodiment of this Quranic truth, making the two halves of the testimony a single reality in the life of a believer [10]. This declaration is so fundamental that it is described as the "most important truth that God revealed to mankind" [2]. To truly adhere to this testimony is to follow the Prophet's example in all facets of life [9, 10].

Qur’ān 21:25  This conveys that all forms of worship, whether it be praying, fasting, invoking, seeking refuge in, and offering an animal as sacrifice, must be directed to God and to God alone. Directing any form of worship to other than God (whether it be an angel, a messenger, Jesus, Muhammad, a saint, an idol, the sun, the moon, a tree) is seen as a contradiction to the fundamental message of Islām, and it is an unforgivable sin unless it is repented from before one dies. All forms of worship must be directed to God only.  Worship means the performance of deeds and sayings that please God, things which He com-manded or encouraged to be performed, either by direct textual proof or by analogy. Thus, wor-ship is not restricted to the implementation of the five pillars of Islām, but also includes every as-pect of life. Providing food for one’s family, and saying something pleasant to cheer a person up are also considered acts of worship, if such is done with the intention of pleasing God. This means that, to be accepted, all acts of worship must be carried out sincerely for the Sake of God alone.  The second part of the testimony means that Prophet Muhammad is the servant and chosen mes-senger of God. This implies that one obeys and follows the commands of the Prophet. One must believe in what he has said, practice his teachings and avoid what he has forbidden. One must therefore worship God only according to his teaching alone, for all the teachings of the Prophet were in fact revelations and inspirations conveyed to him by God.  One must try to mold their lives and character and emulate the Prophet, as he was a living example for humans to follow. God says:

Directing any form of worship to other than God (whether it be an angel, a messenger, Jesus, Muhammad, a saint, an idol, the sun, the moon, a tree) is seen as a contradiction to the fundamental message of Islām, and it is an unforgivable sin unless it is repented from before one dies. All forms of worship must be directed to God only.  Worship means the performance of deeds and sayings that please God, things which He com-manded or encouraged to be performed, either by direct textual proof or by analogy. Thus, wor-ship is not restricted to the implementation of the five pillars of Islām, but also includes every as-pect of life. Providing food for one’s family, and saying something pleasant to cheer a person up are also considered acts of worship, if such is done with the intention of pleasing God. This means that, to be accepted, all acts of worship must be carried out sincerely for the Sake of God alone.  The second part of the testimony means that Prophet Muhammad is the servant and chosen mes-senger of God. This implies that one obeys and follows the commands of the Prophet. One must believe in what he has said, practice his teachings and avoid what he has forbidden. One must therefore worship God only according to his teaching alone, for all the teachings of the Prophet were in fact revelations and inspirations conveyed to him by God.  One must try to mold their lives and character and emulate the Prophet, as he was a living example for humans to follow. God says:

Qur’ān 21:25  This conveys that all forms of worship, whether it be praying, fasting, invoking, seeking refuge in, and offering an animal as sacrifice, must be directed to God and to God alone.
